title: E-6.7: Decision doc: Parsley vs hand-rolled parser
description:
Context:
- Krueger currently uses Parsley as its parser combinator library. Before investing in readability cleanup (E-6.2, E-6.3, E-6.4) and diagnostics/recovery work (E-6.5, E-6.6), the team needs a documented go/no-go decision on whether Parsley remains the right substrate or whether a hand-rolled Elm parser would better serve the project's goals (error recovery, incremental parsing, Kyo integration, cross-platform Scala.js/Native support). This task runs early to inform all downstream tasks.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-E67-001 (When): When this task is closed, the system shall have a written decision document (ADR or equivalent) recommending either continuing with Parsley or switching to a hand-rolled parser, with explicit rationale.
- REQ-E67-002 (If): If the recommendation is to keep Parsley, then the document shall also outline how Parsley integrates with Kyo effects and error-recovery hooks.
- REQ-E67-003 (If): If the recommendation is to replace Parsley, then the document shall outline the replacement strategy, migration path, and estimated effort for E-6.2 through E-6.6.
- REQ-E67-004 (Where): Where the decision can assume Kyo + ParseContext (E-6.1 complete), the evaluation shall factor in Env[ParseContext] threading compatibility.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Produce a decision document (ADR markdown or bd design note) committed to the repo or attached to this issue.
   - Evaluate: error-recovery support, incremental-parsing hooks, Kyo effect integration, cross-platform compatibility (JVM/JS/Native), maintainability, community/ecosystem.
   - Document must include a clear recommendation with pros/cons table.
   - No production code changes in this task; decision only.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: decision document is complete, well-structured, and addresses all evaluation criteria.
   - negative/failure: if evidence is insufficient for a firm recommendation, the document shall clearly state the open questions and propose a time-boxed spike.
   - edge/boundary: evaluation covers the Scala Native target (not just JVM/JS).
   - regression: no existing parser behavior changes as a result of this task.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - No user-visible change; internal planning artifact only.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Decision document is version-controlled and its rationale is reproducible by any team member reading it cold.

title: K-1.2: Service pattern conventions doc + base traits
description:
Context:
- Document and implement the Kyo service pattern conventions across all Krueger modules. Saved at docs/conventions/kyo-services.md with a worked example. Also wires the standard logging substrate: scribe (com.outr::scribe :: 3.16.1, cross JVM/JS/Native) bridged to Kyo's Log effect via a small ScribeLogHandler in krueger.core.log. Service code emits diagnostics via Env[Log]; production layers wire the scribe-backed handler; tests wire an in-memory recorder. No published kyo-scribe bridge exists today, so the handler is in-house and intentionally narrow (~50 LOC of glue) to ease replacement when an upstream bridge ships.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-K12-001 (When): When a developer creates a new Kyo service, the system shall provide base traits and a documented pattern to follow, including how to declare an Env[Log] dependency.
- REQ-K12-002 (If): If docs/conventions/kyo-services.md is present, then the system shall include a runnable worked example with effects, layers, Env[Log], and a test pattern showing the in-memory recorder.
- REQ-K12-003 (While): While service implementations evolve, the system shall keep base traits and ScribeLogHandler backwards-compatible.
- REQ-K12-004 (When): When a Kyo Log emission flows through the production layer, the system shall route it to scribe via ScribeLogHandler with level + structured context preserved.
- REQ-K12-005 (If): If a test wires the in-memory log recorder layer instead of scribe, then no scribe sinks shall be invoked and the recorder shall capture every emission for assertion.
- REQ-K12-006 (Where): Where the layer providing Log is absent, the system shall fail with a clear missing-dependency message at composition time.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - docs/conventions/kyo-services.md with EARS-structured content covering services, layers, AND Env[Log]
   - Base service trait(s) in appropriate module (compiler-api or core)
   - Worked example demonstrating effect composition with < S syntax and Env[Log] use
   - Add com.outr::scribe :: 3.16.1 to mill-build/src/build/Modules.scala (cross JVM/JS/Native)
   - Add ScribeLogHandler in krueger.core.log implementing the Kyo Log handler contract
   - Provide a default ScribeLogLayer for production wiring and an InMemoryLogRecorderLayer for tests
2) Test matrix:
   - happy-path: compile base traits; example compiles with zero warnings; scribe handler emits at every level when invoked through Kyo Log
   - negative/failure: missing Log layer surfaces a clear composition-time error; wrong layer injection fails at composition time
   - edge/boundary: empty effect stack; structured context (key/value pairs) round-trips through to scribe; cross-platform behavior on JVM/JS/Native
   - regression: no library-side coupling to scribe leaks beyond the handler module; no existing service broken
3) User-visible behavior:
   - docs site page for kyo-services.md
   - CLI consumers (later) can configure scribe sinks (stdout, file, JSON) without touching library code
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Example compiles with zero warnings
   - Log emission ordering is deterministic
   - Handler errors carry stable, actionable text
close_reason: Closed

title: K-1.1: Add Kyo SNAPSHOT deps + Sonatype resolver to Mill build
description:
Context:
- Wire io.getkyo:kyo-core/kyo-prelude/kyo-direct/kyo-combinators (minimum required version >= 1.0-RC1+214-534321a9-SNAPSHOT) and the Sonatype snapshots resolver into mill-build/src/build/Modules.scala. Apply across core, trees, compiler-api, testkit, cli. Cross JVM/JS/Native where supported.
- Reason for the lower bound: 1.0-RC1+214-534321a9-SNAPSHOT is the build that introduced kyo-schema on the Kyo mainline. Later snapshot or stable releases are acceptable; pin the floor, not the ceiling.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-K11-001 (When): When mill resolves dependencies, the system shall fetch Kyo artifacts at a version >= 1.0-RC1+214-534321a9-SNAPSHOT from the Sonatype snapshots resolver (or Maven Central for stable releases).
- REQ-K11-002 (If): If a target is JVM-only, then the system shall not attempt to resolve kyo-direct or kyo-combinators for JS/Native targets that do not support them.
- REQ-K11-003 (While): While mill-build/src/build/Modules.scala is the single source of dependency declarations, the system shall define all Kyo version constants there using a single shared minimum-version constant.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Add Sonatype snapshots resolver to mill-build/src/build/Modules.scala
   - Declare a single shared Kyo minimum version constant (>= 1.0-RC1+214-534321a9-SNAPSHOT) and reuse across kyo-core, kyo-prelude, kyo-direct, kyo-combinators
   - Apply deps to core, trees, compiler-api modules; scaffold for testkit/cli
   - Gate JS/Native exclusions per module capability matrix
2) Test matrix: happy-path mill compile, negative below-minimum-version build error, edge JS target missing kyo-direct, regression existing tests still pass
3) User-visible behavior: mill compile succeeds; mill resolve shows Kyo artifacts at or above the minimum
4) Determinism and diagnostics: lockfile (or equivalent) records the resolved version per build for reproducibility; bumping the minimum version is an explicit, reviewed change
close_reason: Closed

title: EPIC-1: Kyo Foundation
description:
Context:
- Introduce Kyo as the core effect system dependency across all Krueger modules (core, trees, compiler-api, future testkit and cli). Kyo provides structured concurrency, resource safety, and composable effects that replace ad-hoc error handling patterns.
- Minimum required Kyo version: >= 1.0-RC1+214-534321a9-SNAPSHOT. Reason for the lower bound: that build introduced kyo-schema on the Kyo mainline, which K-1.6 depends on. Later snapshot or stable releases are acceptable.
- PR #23 (post-filing) landed krueger.trees.unist (UnistNode / UnistProjection driven by QueryableTree[T]) plus CstUnistProjection / AstUnistProjection. K-1.3 is reframed as Kyo-aware visitor variants alongside the existing pure visitors plus a KyoQueryableTree extension; pure visitors retain their cursor capability for upcoming AST/CST transforms, code writers, and semantic analysis.
- scribe (com.outr::scribe :: 3.16.1) is the canonical logging substrate for all Krueger modules. It is bridged to Kyo's Log effect via ScribeLogHandler, delivered by K-1.2. Service code declares Env[Log] dependencies; production layers wire the scribe-backed handler; tests wire an in-memory recorder.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-KYO-EPIC-001 (When): When any Krueger module is built, the system shall resolve Kyo dependencies at version >= 1.0-RC1+214-534321a9-SNAPSHOT via the Sonatype snapshots resolver (or Maven Central for stable releases).
- REQ-KYO-EPIC-002 (If): If a visitor or compiler stage exposes a Kyo-aware variant, then it shall accept callbacks of shape Node => A < S; pure visitors remain unchanged for callers that do not need effects.
- REQ-KYO-EPIC-003 (While): While cross-platform targets (JVM/JS/Native) are active, the system shall apply only the Kyo modules supported by each target.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Mill build wired with Kyo (>= 1.0-RC1+214-534321a9-SNAPSHOT) + Sonatype snapshots resolver across core/trees/compiler-api and scaffolding for testkit/cli
   - Service pattern conventions documented (docs/conventions/kyo-services.md)
   - Kyo-aware visitor variants added alongside the existing pure visitors; pure visitors keep their cursor API
   - KyoQueryableTree extension over QueryableTree[T] for effect-tracked traversal
   - Stage[I, O, S] abstraction added in compiler-api
   - scribe (>= 3.16.1) added cross JVM/JS/Native; ScribeLogHandler bridges Kyo Log to scribe; service-pattern doc covers Env[Log] usage
2) Test matrix: happy-path build on JVM/JS/Native, negative below-minimum-version build error, edge cross-platform matrix, regression no-behavior-change for existing parser/query/unist suites
3) User-visible behavior: build compiles and tests pass on all supported targets; library API gains Kyo-aware surface alongside pure surface
4) Determinism and diagnostics: reproducible resolver order, clear error when below the minimum version, Kyo-aware visitor traversal order matches pure variant for identical inputs

title: Fix CI red on PR #18: scalafmt + writeToWasmSite test deps
description:
PR #18 has two blocking CI failures:
1. Lint: krueger/core/src/io/eleven19/krueger/lexer/ElmTokenizer.scala is misformatted (run scalafmt)
2. Build & Test: ChicoryCompilerWasmCompatibilityTest.`writeToWasmSite copies supported compiler Wasm artifacts` fails because sites/try-wasm/static/wasm/facade/main.js + webgc/main.wasm aren't produced before krueger.itest runs on CI.

Fix both, push, iterate until green.
close_reason: Fixed scalafmt drift on ElmTokenizer + 5 itest files; added webapp-wasm.writeToWasmSite step before krueger.itest. PR #18 CI now green across Lint, Build & Test (JVM/Scala.js/Scala Native).

title: compiler-api: extract shared CompilerComponent surface
description:
Context:
- No cross-platform compiler API module exists; each platform (JVM, JS, Native, WASM) has no shared contract surface to depend on.
- Both playground UIs (Laminar and SvelteKit/WASM) need a stable, platform-agnostic CompilerComponent trait to depend on, backed by PureLogic effects.
- Without this module, webapp, webapp-wasm, and the cross-platform equivalence test cannot be built.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-compilerapi-001 (When): When a consumer calls CompilerComponent.parseCst/parseAst/parseQuery/runQuery, the system shall return a CompileEff[A] value backed by PureLogic QueryEffect, wrapping existing Krueger.parseCst, QueryParser.parse, Matcher.matches, and QueryPretty.render calls.
- REQ-compilerapi-002 (If): If the underlying parse or query operation fails, then the system shall accumulate a structured CompileError (sealed ADT: ParseError, QueryError, InternalError) in the PureLogic Result envelope rather than throwing.
- REQ-compilerapi-003 (Where): Where a caller runs the effect via CompilerComponent.run, the system shall return a CompileResult[A] containing context, logs, errors, and value — the Logic type itself never crossing the JS/WASM FFI boundary.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - New krueger/compiler-api/ module cross-compiled to JVM, JS, Native, and WASM.
   - moduleDeps: krueger.core + krueger.trees (PureLogic arrives transitively via trees).
   - Public surface: CompilerComponent trait, CompileError sealed ADT, MatchView case class, CompilerComponent companion with type aliases (CompileCtx, CompileLog, CompileErr, CompileEff[A], CompileResult[A]) and inline run helper.
   - Default implementation Krueger.component wiring existing pure APIs inside PureLogic effects.
   - Mill module registration in mill-build/src/build/Modules.scala and build.mill.yaml.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: valid Elm source produces non-empty CstModule; valid query on that CST produces MatchView list.
   - negative/failure: malformed source produces ParseError in errors; malformed query produces QueryError in errors; value is None/empty and no exception thrown.
   - edge/boundary: empty source, empty query, query matching zero nodes each return well-formed Result with empty value/matches.
   - regression: none yet — this is new.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - Not directly visible; unblocks webapp and webapp-wasm.
   - Unit tests on JVM confirm the surface before JS/WASM builds.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - CompileResult logs and errors arrays are ordered deterministically (append order from PureLogic).
   - CompileError cases carry phase, message, and optional span for actionable diagnostics.
close_reason: Extracted krueger.compiler-api cross-compiled module (JVM+JS+Native). CompilerComponent trait + CompileError sealed ADT + MatchView (moved from itest) + Krueger.component default impl wrapping parseCst/parseAst/QueryParser/Matcher/QueryPretty inside PureLogic effects. 12 tests pass on JVM and JS. WASM variant deferred to -3hy.

title: E-6.6: Error recovery + partial CST
description:
Context:
- The current parser aborts on the first syntax error, making it impossible for a language server to provide squigglies beyond the first mistake or for the compiler to report multiple errors in one pass. This task adds synchronization-point-based error recovery so the parser can skip past error regions, produce a partial CST with error nodes, and continue parsing the rest of the file.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-E66-001 (When): When the parser encounters a syntax error, the system shall attempt recovery by advancing to the nearest synchronization point (e.g., next top-level declaration, next `in`, next `)` or `]` matching an open bracket).
- REQ-E66-002 (If): If recovery succeeds, then the system shall insert an ErrorNode in the partial CST at the failure region and continue parsing.
- REQ-E66-003 (If): If recovery fails to find a synchronization point, then the system shall produce a partial CST up to the failure point and stop.
- REQ-E66-004 (Where): Where ParseMode is Presentation (from ParseContext), the system shall prefer aggressive recovery to maximize partial CST coverage.
- REQ-E66-005 (When): When a partial CST is produced, every ErrorNode shall reference the corresponding ParseDiagnostic (stable code + span from E-6.5).

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Define synchronization points for top-level declarations, let/in blocks, case branches, and bracketed expressions.
   - Introduce ErrorNode CST node type for error regions.
   - Wire recovery into all parser modules.
   - Depends on E-6.5 for ParseDiagnostic type.
   - Cross-platform JVM / JS / Native.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: well-formed input produces zero ErrorNodes and unchanged CST.
   - negative/failure: single error produces partial CST with exactly one ErrorNode at the correct span.
   - negative/failure: multiple errors produce multiple ErrorNodes, one per recovery region.
   - edge/boundary: error in first token, error in last token, entire file is malformed.
   - regression: all existing parser tests pass; no new ErrorNodes introduced for previously-valid inputs.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - Language server (future) can consume partial CST with ErrorNodes to provide squigglies past the first error.
   - CLI `krueger compile` reports all parse errors in one pass rather than stopping at the first.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Recovery is deterministic: identical malformed input always produces identical partial CST structure.
   - Each ErrorNode carries a stable diagnostic code for the triggering error.

title: E-6.5: Diagnostics: spans, expected sets, stable codes
description:
Context:
- Current parse errors are minimal: they lack source span information, do not report the set of tokens the parser expected, provide no actionable suggestions, and have no stable diagnostic codes. This makes it hard to surface useful squigglies in a language server or to write regression tests against specific error messages. This task upgrades the diagnostic layer to carry source spans, expected token sets, suggestions, and stable codes.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-E65-001 (When): When the parser emits a diagnostic, the system shall include the source span (start offset, end offset, line, column) of the failing token or production.
- REQ-E65-002 (When): When the parser reaches a syntax error, the system shall include the set of expected tokens or production names at the failure point.
- REQ-E65-003 (If): If a common mistake pattern is recognized (e.g., missing `in` after `let`), then the diagnostic shall include a human-readable suggestion.
- REQ-E65-004 (Where): Where diagnostics are emitted, each shall carry a stable alphanumeric code (e.g., ELM-P001) that does not change between compiler versions.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Define a ParseDiagnostic type with: code (stable String), span (SourceSpan), message (String), expectedTokens (List[TokenType]), suggestion (Option[String]).
   - Update all parse error sites in lexer/tokenizer/parser to produce ParseDiagnostic instead of raw exceptions or strings.
   - Assign stable codes to all current error sites.
   - Cross-platform JVM / JS / Native.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: successful parse produces zero diagnostics.
   - negative/failure: known error patterns each produce the expected stable code, span, and message.
   - edge/boundary: error at end-of-file, error in first token, multi-error input (all errors reported, not just first).
   - regression: all existing error-path tests updated to assert on stable codes rather than raw strings.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - CLI output of `krueger compile` shall display source spans and stable codes for parse errors after this task.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Diagnostic codes are immutable once assigned; changes require a new code, not a rename.
   - Span information is deterministic for identical inputs.

title: E-6.4: Parser readability + production-rule visibility
description:
Context:
- The Elm parser (ModuleParser, DeclarationParser, ExpressionParser, PatternParser) has grown in an ad-hoc manner. Production rules are not clearly named after the corresponding EBNF grammar productions, making it hard to verify completeness, trace parse failures, and onboard contributors. This task applies a readability refactor across all parser modules so the EBNF-style grammar is obvious from the code structure, and it integrates the ParseContext threading introduced by E-6.1.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-E64-001 (When): When a developer reads any parser module, each parser combinator or method shall correspond to a named production in the Elm grammar specification.
- REQ-E64-002 (If): If a parser combinator deviates from the spec grammar for a justified reason (e.g., precedence climbing, layout rule), then a scaladoc comment shall document the deviation.
- REQ-E64-003 (Where): Where ParseContext is available via Env[ParseContext], parser rules shall consult it for mode-specific behavior (e.g., Presentation vs Normal).
- REQ-E64-004 (When): When the refactor is applied, the system shall produce byte-for-byte identical parse output for all existing test fixtures.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Rename parser combinators and methods in ModuleParser, DeclarationParser, ExpressionParser, PatternParser to match Elm spec EBNF production names.
   - Add scaladoc to each production with spec section reference.
   - Wire Env[ParseContext] into each parser module (depends on E-6.1).
   - Informed by E-6.7 decision (Parsley retained or replaced).
   - Remove dead code and duplicated helpers.
   - Cross-platform JVM / JS / Native.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: all existing parser tests pass with identical CST output before and after rename.
   - negative/failure: intentionally malformed input continues to produce the same parse errors as before.
   - edge/boundary: deeply nested expressions, let-in blocks, case expressions with many branches.
   - regression: no new compiler warnings; no behavioral diff vs pre-refactor baseline.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - No user-visible change; internal refactor only.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Parser output is deterministic and unchanged for all existing fixtures.

title: E-6.3: ElmTokenizer readability + EBNF alignment
description:
Context:
- ElmTokenizer, like ElmLexer, has drifted from the Elm EBNF grammar production names. Token type names, combinator helpers, and internal phases are inconsistently named. This task applies the same class of readability refactor as E-6.2 but targets the tokenizer layer: rename token types and production helpers to match grammar, add spec-linked scaladoc, remove dead logic.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-E63-001 (When): When a developer reads ElmTokenizer, each token type and production helper name shall correspond to a named production in the Elm grammar specification.
- REQ-E63-002 (If): If a token type is an implementation artifact with no direct spec counterpart, then a scaladoc comment shall explain its purpose and relationship to the nearest spec production.
- REQ-E63-003 (Where): Where spec section identifiers are stable, scaladoc shall include a reference link or section number.
- REQ-E63-004 (When): When the refactor is applied, the system shall produce byte-for-byte identical tokenizer output for all existing test fixtures.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Rename token types and helper combinators to match Elm spec EBNF production names.
   - Add scaladoc to each token type and combinator with spec section reference.
   - Remove dead code and duplicated helper patterns.
   - Informed by E-6.7 decision (Parsley retained or replaced).
   - Cross-platform JVM / JS / Native.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: all existing tokenizer tests pass with identical output before and after rename.
   - negative/failure: intentionally malformed token sequences continue to produce the same error tokens as before.
   - edge/boundary: operator tokens at grammar boundaries, indent/dedent token production.
   - regression: no new compiler warnings; no behavioral diff vs pre-refactor baseline.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - No user-visible change; internal refactor only.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Tokenizer output is deterministic and unchanged for all existing fixtures.

title: E-6.2: ElmLexer readability + EBNF alignment
description:
Context:
- ElmLexer has grown organically and its rule names no longer match the EBNF grammar productions in the Elm specification. This makes it hard to verify correctness, trace bugs, and onboard new contributors. This task refactors the lexer for clarity: rename rules to match grammar productions, add scaladoc links to spec sections, and remove dead or duplicated logic. The scope is readability only; no behavioral changes.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-E62-001 (When): When a developer reads ElmLexer, each rule name shall correspond directly to a named production in the Elm grammar specification.
- REQ-E62-002 (If): If a rule deviates from the spec for a justified reason, then a scaladoc comment shall document the deviation and its rationale.
- REQ-E62-003 (Where): Where spec section identifiers are stable, scaladoc shall include a reference link or section number.
- REQ-E62-004 (When): When the refactor is applied, the system shall produce byte-for-byte identical lexer output for all existing test fixtures.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Rename lexer rules to match Elm spec EBNF production names.
   - Add scaladoc to each rule with spec section reference.
   - Remove dead code and duplicated helper patterns.
   - Informed by E-6.7 decision (Parsley retained or replaced).
   - Cross-platform JVM / JS / Native.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: all existing lexer tests pass with identical output before and after rename.
   - negative/failure: intentionally malformed input continues to produce the same error tokens as before.
   - edge/boundary: Unicode identifiers, multi-line strings, nested comments at boundary lengths.
   - regression: no new compiler warnings; no behavioral diff vs pre-refactor baseline.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - No user-visible change; internal refactor only.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Lexer output is deterministic and unchanged for all existing fixtures.

title: E-6.1: ParseContext + config threading via Env[ParseContext]
description:
Context:
- The lexer, tokenizer, and parser currently have no uniform mechanism for threading compiler configuration (e.g., normal vs presentation compiler mode). Introducing ParseContext threaded via Kyo's Env[ParseContext] effect gives every production rule access to config without global state and unlocks the presentation-compiler mode needed for language-server squigglies.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-E61-001 (When): When a parse operation is initiated, the system shall make ParseContext (including ParseMode = Normal | Presentation) available to every lexer, tokenizer, and parser production rule via Env[ParseContext].
- REQ-E61-002 (If): If ParseMode is Presentation, then the system shall relax fatal-error policy to allow partial results.
- REQ-E61-003 (Where): Where existing callers do not supply a ParseContext, the system shall default to ParseMode = Normal with sensible defaults so existing call sites remain source-compatible.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Introduce ParseContext case class with at minimum a ParseMode field.
   - Thread Env[ParseContext] through ElmLexer, ElmTokenizer, and all parser modules (ModuleParser, DeclarationParser, ExpressionParser, PatternParser).
   - Provide a default ParseContext.normal and ParseContext.presentation smart constructor.
   - Cross-platform JVM / JS / Native.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: parse with explicit Normal context produces same output as legacy call site.
   - happy-path: parse with Presentation context does not throw on first error.
   - negative/failure: missing Env[ParseContext] is a compile-time error, not a runtime failure.
   - edge/boundary: ParseContext with unexpected extension fields is forward-compatible.
   - regression: all existing parser / lexer / tokenizer tests pass without modification.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - No CLI-visible change at this stage; internal plumbing only.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - ParseContext must not introduce non-determinism; identical inputs with identical context produce identical outputs.

title: EPIC-6: Elm lexer/tokenizer/parser cleanup + diagnostics + recovery
description:
Context:
- The Elm lexer, tokenizer, and parser have grown messy. They lack a uniform context for threading config (notably normal vs presentation compiler mode), do not surface EBNF production rules clearly, produce weak error messages, lack error recovery (so the compiler falls apart on the first failure), and do not yet support incremental reparses. This epic plans the cleanup, decides whether Parsley remains the right substrate, and lays groundwork for squigglies, language-server support, and incremental parsing.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-EPIC6-001 (When): When all sub-tasks E-6.1 through E-6.8 are closed, the system shall surface a clean, Kyo-aware, diagnostics-capable Elm parser with error recovery and an incremental-parsing design in place.
- REQ-EPIC6-002 (If): If any sub-task is open, then this epic shall remain open.
- REQ-EPIC6-003 (Where): Where sub-tasks produce go/no-go decision documents, the decisions shall be reflected in subsequent sub-task implementations.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - All sub-tasks E-6.1 through E-6.8 completed per their own acceptance criteria.
   - ParseContext threaded through lexer/tokenizer/parser via Env[ParseContext].
   - EBNF-aligned naming in lexer, tokenizer, and parser modules.
   - Diagnostics with spans, expected sets, and stable codes.
   - Error recovery producing partial CST.
   - Incremental parsing design documented.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: full parse pipeline with ParseContext threading.
   - negative/failure: error recovery over malformed input produces partial CST with diagnostics.
   - edge/boundary: empty input, single-token input, deeply nested expressions.
   - regression: all existing parser tests green throughout.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - Compiler CLI surfaces improved error messages with source spans after E-6.5.
   - Language server can survive past the first error after E-6.6.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Diagnostic codes are stable across compiler runs.
   - Parse output is deterministic for identical inputs.

title: C-4.4: krueger parse subcommand
description:
Context:
- Implement the krueger parse subcommand that prints the CST sexp for each source file. Supports --json [path] for machine-readable output and --show-fields to include field names in output.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-C44-001 (When): When krueger parse <source-files...> is run, the system shall print the CST sexp for each file.
- REQ-C44-002 (If): If --show-fields is given, then the system shall include field names in the sexp output (Field variant).
- REQ-C44-003 (If): If --json [path] is given, then the system shall write a JSON object mapping file path to sexp string.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:

title: S-2.5: Cst.toSexp and Ast.toSexp adapters
description:
Context:

EARS requirements:

title: S-2.4: SexpDiff structural diff to hunks for terminal rendering
description:
Context:

title: K-1.4: Compiler pipeline Stage[I,O,S] abstraction
description:
Context:
- Introduce a composable Stage[I, O, S] type alias or trait in compiler-api that represents a pipeline step returning O < S. Enables type-safe composition of parse, validate, and transform stages.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-K14-001 (When): When two Stage values are composed, the system shall produce a new Stage with the combined effect row S1 & S2.
- REQ-K14-002 (If): If a stage fails with an Abort effect, then the system shall propagate the failure through the composed pipeline.

4) Determinism and diagnostics: compile-time errors for invalid compositions; no runtime overhead

title: K-1.3: Kyo-aware visitor variant + KyoQueryableTree extension
description:
Context:
- Tree traversal in Krueger now spans two patterns: the older visitor model (CstVisitor / AstVisitor / QueryVisitor) and the newer typeclass-driven QueryableTree + UnistProjection that landed in PR #23. Visitors retain a powerful cursor capability that the typeclass model does not match, and that cursor capability is essential for the upcoming AST/CST transformation passes, code writers, and semantic-analysis work. Rather than retiring visitors, this task adds Kyo-aware variants alongside the existing pure visitors so callers can opt into effect-tracked traversal without losing cursor ergonomics. It also extends QueryableTree[T] with Kyo-friendly traversal helpers so library consumers can stay in the typeclass world when they want to.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-kyo-visitor-001 (When): When a caller traverses a tree using a Kyo-aware visitor, the system shall accept callbacks of shape `Node => A < S` and propagate the effect set S through traversal.
- REQ-kyo-visitor-002 (If): If an existing pure visitor caller is unchanged, then it shall continue to compile and behave identically.
- REQ-kyo-visitor-003 (Where): Where a Kyo-aware visitor exposes a cursor, the cursor shall provide the same parent / child / sibling navigation operations as the pure visitor cursor, returning effect-tracked results.
- REQ-kyo-visitor-004 (When): When a caller uses `KyoQueryableTree[T]` extension methods, the system shall offer effect-tracked equivalents of the existing pure traversal operations on QueryableTree[T].

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Add KyoCstVisitor, KyoAstVisitor, KyoQueryVisitor parallel to the existing pure visitors. Share span/cursor types where possible.
   - Add a KyoQueryableTree extension over QueryableTree[T] giving `A < S` traversal helpers (e.g., foldKyo, traverseKyo).
   - Existing CstVisitor / AstVisitor / QueryVisitor remain unchanged in behavior; mark in scaladoc that they are the pure variant.
   - Cross-platform JVM / JS / Native.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path: Kyo-aware visitor over a representative CST + AST tree composes with at least IO and Abort callbacks.
   - negative/failure: Abort.fail inside a callback short-circuits traversal cleanly with a stable diagnostic.
   - edge/boundary: empty tree, single-node tree, deeply nested tree (stack-safety), cursor navigation around boundaries (root parent, leaf child).
   - regression: every pre-existing visitor / queryable test stays green.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - Library API only at this stage; no CLI consequences.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Traversal order is deterministic and matches the pure visitor order for the same inputs.
   - Failure diagnostics carry stable, actionable text including the failing node type when applicable.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add unist tree projection and AST Explorer playground treeview
description:
Context:
- The playground needs an AST Explorer-style treeview for CST and AST output.
- Existing parseCst/parseAst values are opaque Scala handles, so the browser cannot inspect tree structure.
- Add a unifiedjs-style unist projection layer, expose CST/AST unist facade methods, and consume them in the Svelte playground treeview in one PR.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-unist-treeview-001 (When): When a CST or AST root is projected, the system shall emit a JSON-compatible unist tree preserving node type, text, position, children, and field metadata.
- REQ-unist-treeview-002 (If): If parsing fails, then the system shall preserve the existing compiler error envelope shape and render human-readable errors in the playground.
- REQ-unist-treeview-003 (Where): Where JS and WebGC compiler facades are used, the system shall expose matching parseCstUnist and parseAstUnist methods with identical plain-object shapes.
- REQ-unist-treeview-004 (When): When the playground displays CST or AST output, the system shall render an AST Explorer-style collapsible tree with search, expand/collapse controls, node text, child counts, and raw fallback.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Add krueger.trees unist model and projection type class on top of QueryableTree.
   - Add CST and AST projection instances reusing existing QueryableTree children/fields/text behavior.
   - Add parseCstUnist and parseAstUnist to JS and WebGC facades.
   - Update try-wasm TypeScript client and TreeView.svelte to consume unist trees.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path coverage for toy, CST, AST, facade, and UI tree rendering
   - negative/failure coverage for malformed CST/AST parsing and UI error rendering
   - edge/boundary coverage for empty children, empty fields, zero-length spans, raw fallback, and no search matches
   - regression coverage proving QueryableTree order/fields remain stable and existing query execution still uses opaque handles
3) User-visible behavior:
   - CST and AST panels render navigable tree output in the playground for JS and WebGC backends.
   - Existing Matches, Canonical Query, and Settings panels continue to work.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Field indexes and child ordering are deterministic.
   - Parse and projection failures surface stable, actionable diagnostics.
close_reason: Added unist projection model, CST/AST adapters, JS/WebGC facade exports, TypeScript normalization, AST Explorer-style playground treeview, and matching smoke-test updates.

title: Wire WASM-linked compiler facade so /try-wasm/ WASM mode actually loads main.wasm
description:
Context:
- Today both backends route through the JS-linked compiler-api.js. The compiler-api.wasm artifact is deployed but never executed at runtime. Goal: have WASM mode load main.wasm and run compile calls through it, so the runtime path actually diverges.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-wasm-runtime-001 (When): When the user selects 'WASM' in the playground, the system shall fetch /try-wasm/wasm/webgc/main.wasm and route compile calls through that artifact.
- REQ-wasm-runtime-002 (When): When the WASM module finishes initialising, the system shall expose a globalThis.KruegerWasm object with parseCst/parseAst/parseQuery/runQuery/prettyQuery/tokenize whose envelope shape matches the JS facade.
- REQ-wasm-runtime-003 (If): If WASM-GC is unavailable in the host browser, the system shall not attempt to fetch main.wasm and shall surface the graceful JS-fallback behaviour already in place.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Add krueger.webapp-wasm.wasm Mill submodule (Scala.js WASM linker) sharing webapp-wasm/src; new WasmMain registers globalThis.KruegerWasm via js.Dynamic.global on module init.
   - writeToWasmSite + docs.copyWasmArtifactsToTryWasm produce wasm/webgc/main.js+main.wasm from the new submodule (replacing the no-op compiler-api.wasm copy).
   - lib/krueger.ts loads wasm/facade/main.js for backend='js' and wasm/webgc/main.js for backend='webgc'; KruegerClient destroys+recreates on switch.
   - BackendLoader/KruegerJs.setBackend become vestigial in the JS-linked module; remove or leave only the cosmetic id reporting.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy: headless browser asserts globalThis.KruegerWasm.parseCst returns a valid envelope after wasm load.
   - negative: malformed source through WASM facade returns ok=false envelope (parity with JS facade).
   - edge: facade switch back+forth round-trips without leaking listeners or state.
   - regression: existing 22+24+31 tests stay green.
3) User-visible behaviour:
   - Playwright e2e asserts a main.wasm fetch occurs only for the WASM pass.
4) Determinism/diagnostics:
   - WASM-load failures surface as a structured CompilerEnvelope error in the existing UI banner.
close_reason: WASM mode now actually loads main.wasm and runs compile calls through the WebAssembly artifact via the new krueger.webapp-wasm.wasm Mill submodule (Scala.js Wasm linker) and a top-level KruegerWasm export. Verified with Node --experimental-wasm-exnref smoke. Tests: 18 webapp-wasm + 31 try-wasm Vitest pass.

title: Eliminate Laminar playground; add WASM/JS backend selector to Svelte playground
description:
Context:
- The Astro docs ship two playgrounds today: '/try/' (Laminar+Scala.js) and '/try-wasm/' (SvelteKit). The user wants to drop Laminar entirely and consolidate on the Svelte UI, while still exercising both a WASM and a JS compiler backend through a runtime selector.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-playground-consolidate-001 (When): When the docs are built, the system shall not include any Laminar/'/try/' page or 'krueger.webapp' module artifact.
- REQ-playground-consolidate-002 (When): When a user opens the Svelte playground, the system shall expose a UI control to choose a compiler backend ('WASM' or 'JS') and apply the choice to subsequent compile calls.
- REQ-playground-consolidate-003 (If): If WebAssembly GC is not supported by the browser, then the system shall default to the JS backend and disable the WASM option with a clear note.
- REQ-playground-consolidate-004 (Where): Where the previous nav exposed a 'Try ▾' dropdown with two entries, the nav shall expose a single 'Try' link to the unified Svelte playground.

Acceptance:

title: Add Scala.js compiler API acceptance parity
description:
Context:

- Scala.js acceptance parity is intentionally deferred until KruegerJs is routed through the shared CompilerBackend/invoke surface.

- REQ-SJSCAPI-001 (When): When the Scala.js compiler backend receives a valid compiler request, the system shall return a successful compiler response equivalent to JVM/Chicory semantics.
- REQ-SJSCAPI-002 (If): If the Scala.js compiler backend receives malformed Elm source, then the system shall return structured compiler errors equivalent to JVM/Chicory semantics.
- REQ-SJSCAPI-003 (Where): Where compiler API acceptance scenarios run across supported backends, the system shall include Scala.js parity coverage without depending on JVM-only Cucumber execution.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:

   - Choose either a JS-side Gherkin runner or shared scenario fixtures consumed by Scala.js tests.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):

title: compiler-api: add canonical invoke contract
description:
Context:

- REQ-compilerapi-invoke-001 (When): When a caller invokes parseCst with valid source JSON, the system shall return a deterministic success envelope encoded as canonical JSON.
- REQ-compilerapi-invoke-002 (If): If parseCst receives malformed source input, then the system shall return a structured parse error envelope encoded as JSON without throwing.
- REQ-compilerapi-invoke-003 (Where): Where a caller requests an unknown operation, the system shall return a structured internal error envelope with a stable diagnostic message.

Acceptance:

title: compiler-api ABI: batch operations across the Wasm boundary
description:
Context:
- The planned canonical host-callable ABI uses UTF-8 JSON over explicit linear-memory exports such as alloc/free/invoke.
- Calling the ABI once per compiler operation will add avoidable host/guest crossing overhead when a client needs to parse multiple modules, canonicalize queries, and compute several outputs in sequence.
- We need a follow-up expansion that supports batched multi-source and multi-output requests while preserving the single-operation ABI as the minimal stable baseline.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-compilerapiabi-batch-001 (When): When a host submits a batched request containing multiple sources and/or requested outputs, the system shall evaluate the requested operations in one ABI invocation and return a single deterministic JSON response containing all requested results.
- REQ-compilerapiabi-batch-002 (If): If one item in a batched request fails to parse or execute, then the system shall return structured diagnostics for that item without corrupting unrelated successful results in the same batch.
- REQ-compilerapiabi-batch-003 (Where): Where a batched request is semantically equivalent to N individual invoke calls, the system shall return byte-equivalent per-item result payloads in a deterministic order.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Extend the canonical ABI request/response schema to support multiple source units and multiple requested outputs per invocation.
   - Preserve backward compatibility for the initial single-operation invoke contract.
   - Define deterministic ordering rules for batched results and diagnostics.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path coverage
   - negative/failure coverage
   - edge/boundary coverage
   - regression coverage
3) User-visible behavior:
   - Browser and JVM hosts can reduce boundary crossings without changing higher-level semantics.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Batched responses preserve request order or another explicitly documented stable order.
   - Per-item failures remain actionable and isolated.

title: compiler-api-wasi: Chicory-loadable fallback artifact
description:
Context:
- Direct Chicory instantiation of the Scala.js-generated `compiler-api.wasm` bundle is currently blocked by required JavaScript host imports (`__scalaJSHelpers`, `wasm:js-string`, imported string constants) surfaced by the generated Scala.js loader.
- The handoff plan already identified a Scala Native WASI fallback path if the direct WasmGC route proves unsuitable for JVM-side validation.
- We need a JVM-loadable WebAssembly artifact that exercises the same compiler-api behaviors through Chicory without depending on a browser or JS host runtime.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-compilerapiwasi-001 (When): When the fallback WASI artifact is built and loaded through Chicory, the system shall expose callable compiler-api wrapper exports for parse/query operations without requiring JavaScript host imports.
- REQ-compilerapiwasi-002 (If): If malformed source or query input is passed through the fallback artifact, then the system shall return stable structured diagnostics across the WASM boundary rather than throwing host exceptions.
- REQ-compilerapiwasi-003 (Where): Where the same fixture is executed through JVM compiler-api and the fallback WASI artifact, the system shall produce byte-equivalent serialized results for the covered entrypoints.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Add a Scala Native WASM/WASI module or equivalent fallback artifact dedicated to Chicory/JVM validation.
   - Export C-compatible wrapper functions for the required compiler-api behaviors.
   - Keep the fallback scoped to JVM-side validation; browser runtime wiring continues to use the Scala.js Wasm artifact.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path coverage: valid Elm source/query succeeds through the fallback artifact.
   - negative/failure coverage: malformed inputs surface structured errors with no host crash.
   - edge/boundary coverage: empty input and repeated calls remain deterministic.
   - regression coverage: serialized fallback results match JVM compiler-api for the same fixtures.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - Indirectly user-visible via trustworthy JVM-side WASM validation.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Export names and buffer protocol are stable.
   - Test failures identify the fallback artifact path and wrapper export used.

title: compiler-api: Chicory integration test for emitted WASM artifact
description:
Context:
- The WASM playground plan needs JVM-side validation that the emitted `compiler-api.wasm` binary is loadable and callable independently of the browser runtime.
- The first compatibility probe should use Chicory against the actual linked artifact and record whether WasmGC is supported in the chosen runtime version.
- This work depends on `compiler-api.wasm` emitting a real artifact set that the JVM tests can locate.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-compilerapi-chicory-001 (When): When the JVM Chicory integration suite runs against the emitted `compiler-api.wasm` artifact, the system shall load the module, initialize it as required, and exercise callable compiler entrypoints across the WASM boundary.
- REQ-compilerapi-chicory-002 (If): If the selected Chicory runtime does not support the emitted WasmGC module, then the suite shall fail or skip with a precise compatibility diagnostic that identifies the unsupported capability and the runtime version used.
- REQ-compilerapi-chicory-003 (Where): Where the same Elm/query fixture is executed through JVM compiler-api and the WASM artifact, the system shall return byte-for-byte equivalent serialized results for the covered entrypoints.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Add a JVM-only test module that resolves the emitted WASM artifact and probes it through Chicory.
   - Record the runtime version and compatibility outcome in the issue notes/design while implementing.
   - If direct WasmGC execution remains unsupported, file or link the fallback WASI path instead of silently weakening coverage.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path coverage: valid Elm source succeeds through at least one callable WASM compiler entrypoint.
   - negative/failure coverage: malformed Elm or query input returns structured errors through the WASM boundary without throwing host exceptions.
   - edge/boundary coverage: empty input returns the agreed envelope shape; repeated calls are deterministic.
   - regression coverage: WASM results are cross-checked against JVM compiler-api for the same fixtures.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - Indirectly user-visible via stronger confidence in the shipped WASM compiler bundle.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Tests identify the artifact path, exported entrypoint used, and runtime version in failures.
   - Compatibility failures are stable and actionable, not generic linker/runtime crashes.
notes:

- Probe committed in PR #18 (commit 2893ab8) as krueger/itest/src/io/eleven19/krueger/itest/ChicoryCompilerWasmCompatibilityTest.scala (renamed from earlier ChicoryCompilerApiProbeTest.scala referenced in prior notes).

- Observed instantiation: com.dylibso.chicory.wasm.UnlinkableException with 'unknown import ... __scalaJSHelpers.JSTag' (asserted at lines 51-62).

title: compiler-api.wasm: emit loadable WasmGC artifact
description:
Context:
- The current `./mill krueger.compiler-api.wasm.fullLinkJS` task succeeds, but the linker output directory is empty because Mill reports `No main class specified or found`.
- This blocks both browser runtime wiring and JVM-side validation because there is no `main.wasm` or loader to execute.
- The repo needs a deterministic, testable WasmGC artifact from `compiler-api.wasm` before downstream tasks can proceed.

EARS requirements:
- REQ-compilerapiwasm-artifact-001 (When): When `./mill krueger.compiler-api.wasm.fullLinkJS` runs, the system shall emit a non-empty WasmGC artifact set including `main.wasm` and its ES-module loader.
- REQ-compilerapiwasm-artifact-002 (If): If the linker cannot discover a reachable entrypoint, then the build or test shall fail with a clear diagnostic identifying the missing WASM entrypoint rather than succeeding with an empty output directory.
- REQ-compilerapiwasm-artifact-003 (Where): Where the compiler core is consumed from JS or JVM integration tests, the emitted artifact layout shall be stable and discoverable from build output paths.

Acceptance:
1) Implementation expectations:
   - Add the minimal reachable WASM entrypoint/config needed for `compiler-api.wasm` to emit an actual artifact.
   - Keep JS-exported facade concerns out of the WASM backend; this slice is only about producing the underlying module and loader.
   - Document or encode the expected output path so downstream tasks can consume it deterministically.
2) Test matrix (mandatory):
   - happy-path coverage: a build-verified assertion proves `main.wasm` and loader files exist and are non-empty after linking.
   - negative/failure coverage: missing or misconfigured entrypoint fails with an actionable message instead of a silent empty output.
   - edge/boundary coverage: repeated fullLinkJS runs overwrite/refresh the same artifact set deterministically.
   - regression coverage: downstream copy/integration tasks continue to resolve the same artifact layout after future compiler-api changes.
3) User-visible behavior:
   - Indirectly user-visible through the browser playground and JVM integration testability.
4) Determinism and diagnostics:
   - Output file names and lookup path are stable.
   - Failure output names the missing entrypoint or artifact location issue.
notes:
Completed on 2026-04-24:
- Added a minimal shared Scala entrypoint at `io.eleven19.krueger.compiler.wasm.Main` so the Scala.js Wasm linker has a reachable module initializer.
- Added `krueger/compiler-api/test-jvm/check-wasm-artifact.mjs` as a deterministic artifact assertion for `main.js` and `main.wasm`.
- Verified red -> green with:
  1. `./mill krueger.compiler-api.wasm.fullLinkJS && node krueger/compiler-api/test-jvm/check-wasm-artifact.mjs` (initially failed before entrypoint, now passes)

title: ci: build both webapps + SvelteKit static output; stitch into Pages artifact
description:
Context:

- REQ-ci-002 (When): When the SvelteKit build completes, the system shall copy sites/try-wasm/build/ into docs/dist/try-wasm/ so the single Pages artifact upload at docs/dist covers both sites.

   - Add step: cd sites/try-wasm && npm ci && npm run build.
